clinicalStem: A 17-year-old girl presents to the gynecology clinic because she has never had a menstrual period. She is 152 cm tall, weighs 48 kg, and has a webbed neck and wide-set nipples. Breast development is Tanner stage 1. Her external genitalia appear female. Baseline investigations show FSH 68 mIU/mL, LH 54 mIU/mL, and estradiol 8 pg/mL. Which single investigation would BEST confirm the underlying diagnosis?
options: Pelvic ultrasound to assess uterine and ovarian anatomy
explanation: This girl has classic features of Turner syndrome (45,X): short stature, webbed neck, wide-set nipples, primary amenorrhea, and hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (high FSH/LH, low estradiol). Karyotype analysis confirms the chromosomal abnormality. Pelvic ultrasound would show streak gonads but does not confirm the diagnosis. Pituitary MRI is indicated for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, not hypergonadotropic. Prolactin/TSH and progesterone challenge are irrelevant here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pelvic ultrasound would reveal streak gonads and a small uterus, but cannot confirm the karyotypic diagnosis of Turner syndrome.
Pituitary MRI is indicated when FSH and LH are LOW (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism), not when they are markedly elevated as in this case.
Karyotype analysis is correct — it will demonstrate 45,X (or a mosaic variant), directly confirming Turner syndrome.
Serum prolactin and TSH are checked when amenorrhea is associated with galactorrhea or hypothyroid features; this patient's phenotype points clearly to a chromosomal cause.
The progesterone challenge assesses whether the outflow tract is intact and estrogen is adequate; it is not useful when estradiol is already known to be very low.
clinicalPearl: Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism in a phenotypic female with primary amenorrhea and short stature mandates karyotype to exclude Turner syndrome (45,X).
keyClue: Webbed neck, wide-set nipples, Tanner 1 breasts, elevated FSH/LH, low estradiol — hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with somatic features of Turner syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Ordering a pelvic ultrasound first — it may show streak gonads but cannot provide the definitive chromosomal diagnosis.
missedConcept: Distinguishing hypergonadotropic from hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and recognizing the Turner phenotype directing immediate karyotyping.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old competitive marathon runner presents with 8 months of absent periods. She runs approximately 80 km per week, has a BMI of 17.5 kg/m², and reports fatigue and stress fractures of both tibias over the past year. She is not on any medications and denies galactorrhea. Serum hCG is negative. Labs show FSH 3.2 mIU/mL, LH 2.1 mIU/mL, estradiol 22 pg/mL, prolactin 14 ng/mL, TSH 2.1 mIU/L. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Hypothalamic amenorrhea due to functional suppression
explanation: This patient's low BMI, excessive exercise, chronic stress, and low-normal gonadotropins with low estradiol are the hallmark triad of functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (the 'female athlete triad'). The hypothalamus suppresses GnRH pulsatility in response to energy deficit, resulting in low FSH/LH and estrogen. Premature ovarian insufficiency would cause high FSH. Prolactinoma would elevate prolactin. TSH is normal excluding hypothyroidism. PCOS typically shows normal-to-elevated LH and androgen excess.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Premature ovarian insufficiency causes elevated FSH (>25 mIU/mL) due to loss of ovarian feedback; this patient's FSH is low-normal.
Prolactinoma causes hyperprolactinemia (usually >50 ng/mL) and sometimes galactorrhea; prolactin here is normal.
Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea is correct — the classic triad of low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone density is fully present.
Primary hypothyroidism raises TSH; this patient's TSH is normal, excluding this diagnosis.
PCOS is associated with androgen excess, often obesity, and LH:FSH ratio >2; this patient's gonadotropins are both low and her BMI is very low.
clinicalPearl: The female athlete triad — low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and reduced bone density — is caused by functional suppression of hypothalamic GnRH pulsatility; gonadotropins are characteristically low-normal.
keyClue: Marathon runner, BMI 17.5, stress fractures, low FSH and LH with low estradiol — energy-deficit suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
commonExamTrap: Confusing low gonadotropins of hypothalamic amenorrhea with premature ovarian insufficiency, which paradoxically has HIGH gonadotropins.
missedConcept: Recognizing functional (reversible) hypothalamic suppression as distinct from structural or gonadal causes of amenorrhea.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman presents with secondary amenorrhea for 5 months following a surgical curettage performed for incomplete miscarriage. She has no hot flushes, no galactorrhea, and no features of hypothyroidism. Serum hCG, prolactin, TSH, FSH, and LH are all within normal limits. Serum estradiol is 180 pg/mL. A progesterone challenge with medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg daily for 10 days produces NO withdrawal bleed. Which investigation is BEST to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation: The clinical picture — secondary amenorrhea after uterine curettage, normal hormones and estrogen, and absent withdrawal bleed despite adequate estrogen priming — is classic for Asherman syndrome (intrauterine adhesions). Diagnostic hysteroscopy is the gold standard investigation as it directly visualises, characterises, and allows simultaneous treatment of intrauterine adhesions. HSG can suggest adhesions but cannot confirm or treat them. Pelvic MRI and TVS have low sensitivity for intracavitary adhesions. Laparoscopy evaluates the peritoneal cavity, not the uterine cavity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hysterosalpingography can reveal an irregular cavity suggesting adhesions but has limited sensitivity and specificity compared to direct visualisation; it is a second-line imaging modality.
Pelvic MRI is useful for deep endometriosis and structural anomalies but has poor sensitivity for intrauterine adhesions and is not the diagnostic standard.
Diagnostic hysteroscopy is correct — it is the gold standard for visualising and grading intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) and can be therapeutic simultaneously.
Transvaginal ultrasound may show a thin or irregular endometrium but lacks the sensitivity and specificity to diagnose intrauterine adhesions definitively.
Laparoscopy evaluates the peritoneal cavity and adnexa; it does not assess the uterine cavity and is not indicated to diagnose Asherman syndrome.
clinicalPearl: Post-curettage amenorrhea with normal hormones and no withdrawal bleed on progesterone challenge = Asherman syndrome until proven otherwise; hysteroscopy is the gold standard.
keyClue: Post-curettage secondary amenorrhea + normal estradiol + absent progesterone-challenge withdrawal bleed = outflow tract obstruction.
commonExamTrap: Ordering HSG when hysteroscopy is available — HSG is diagnostic imaging only, while hysteroscopy is both diagnostic AND therapeutic.
missedConcept: Recognising that a failed progesterone challenge with adequate endogenous estrogen points to an outflow obstruction, not ovarian or hypothalamic disease.

sourceLecture: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders
sourceFile: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders.pdf
clinicalStem: A newborn is delivered at 38 weeks. The baby has ambiguous genitalia — clitoromegaly, partial labial fusion, and a single perineal opening. The karyotype returns 46,XX. On day 7 of life, the infant presents with poor feeding, vomiting, and lethargy. Serum sodium is 124 mmol/L and potassium is 6.8 mmol/L. What is the MOST LIKELY underlying diagnosis?
explanation: A 46,XX infant with virilised genitalia and salt-wasting crisis (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia) in the first week of life is the classic presentation of 21-hydroxylase deficiency, the most common cause of congenital adrenal hyperplasia. The block in cortisol synthesis diverts precursors to androgens (causing virilisation) and impairs aldosterone synthesis (causing salt wasting). CAIS presents with 46,XY and female phenotype, not ambiguous genitalia. 5-alpha reductase deficiency affects 46,XY individuals. Maternal androgen exposure causes virilisation but not the adrenal crisis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Complete androgen insensitivity syndrome occurs in 46,XY individuals who present with a completely female phenotype and primary amenorrhea at puberty, not ambiguous genitalia in a 46,XX infant.
5-alpha reductase deficiency affects 46,XY individuals causing undervirilisation; it does not present in a 46,XX infant.
21-hydroxylase deficiency is correct — it causes virilisation of 46,XX females and, in the salt-wasting form, life-threatening hyponatraemia and hyperkalaemia in the neonatal period.
True hermaphroditism (ovotesticular DSD) is extremely rare and does not explain the electrolyte crisis; the hormonal picture points specifically to adrenal dysfunction.

Maternal androgen exposure (e.g., from progestins or tumors) causes virilisation of external genitalia but does NOT cause aldosterone deficiency or adrenal crisis; sodium and potassium would be normal.
clinicalPearl: A 46,XX virilised newborn in adrenal crisis (hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia) = 21-hydroxylase deficiency until proven otherwise; this is life-threatening and requires urgent hydrocortisone and saline.
keyClue: 46,XX virilised infant + hyponatraemia 124 mmol/L + hyperkalaemia 6.8 mmol/L in first week of life = salt-wasting CAH.
commonExamTrap: Selecting maternal androgen exposure — this explains the genital ambiguity but cannot explain the electrolyte disturbance.
missedConcept: Connecting ambiguous genitalia in a 46,XX infant to the adrenal salt-wasting crisis as part of 21-hydroxylase deficiency.
redFlagReason: Adrenal crisis with severe hyponatraemia (124 mmol/L) and hyperkalaemia (6.8 mmol/L) in a neonate is immediately life-threatening; delayed diagnosis and treatment causes cardiovascular collapse and death.

sourceLecture: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders
sourceFile: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders.pdf
clinicalStem: A 6-year-old girl is brought by her mother who noticed breast budding 2 months ago. On examination, she has Tanner stage 2 breast development and sparse pubic hair. Her height velocity has increased to 8 cm/year. Bone age X-ray shows advancement of 2 years over chronological age. Basal LH is 1.2 IU/L and FSH is 2.4 IU/L. To confirm the underlying cause and differentiate central from peripheral precocious puberty, which investigation is MOST appropriate NEXT?
options: MRI of the brain and hypothalamus
explanation: Central precocious puberty (CPP) is confirmed by a GnRH (or GnRH analogue) stimulation test demonstrating a pubertal LH response (peak LH >5 IU/L), proving activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Basal gonadotropins can be in the prepubertal range even in CPP, making stimulated levels essential for diagnosis. Brain MRI follows once CPP is confirmed to exclude a hypothalamic or pituitary lesion. Adrenal androgens and 24-hour steroids are used when adrenal pathology is suspected. Pelvic ultrasound is a supportive (not confirmatory) tool.
wrongAnswerExplanations: DHEA-S and androstenedione evaluate adrenal androgen excess (congenital adrenal hyperplasia or adrenal tumor), which typically causes adrenarche without thelarche or advanced bone age from true gonadal activation.
GnRH stimulation test is correct — it definitively proves HPG axis activation (CPP) by showing a pubertal LH surge, distinguishing CPP from peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) causes.
Pelvic ultrasound can show an enlarged uterus and ovarian follicles consistent with estrogenisation, but it is supportive and cannot confirm central versus peripheral origin.
MRI brain is important but comes AFTER confirming CPP with the GnRH test; performing MRI before confirming CPP wastes resources when the diagnosis is not yet established.
24-hour urinary 17-ketosteroids are an older test for adrenal androgen excess and are not used to confirm central precocious puberty.
clinicalPearl: Basal LH and FSH can be prepubertal even in CPP — a GnRH stimulation test (peak LH >5 IU/L) is the gold standard to confirm HPG axis activation.
keyClue: Breast development + pubic hair + advanced bone age at age 6 with borderline basal gonadotropins — stimulation testing needed to confirm central activation.
commonExamTrap: Proceeding directly to brain MRI without first confirming CPP with the GnRH stimulation test.
missedConcept: Basal gonadotropins may not distinguish CPP from prepubertal; the GnRH stimulation test is required before imaging.

sourceLecture: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders
sourceFile: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders.pdf
clinicalStem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother with a 3-week history of vaginal discharge, itching, and redness around the vaginal opening. The mother reports the child wipes back to front after using the toilet. There is no history of trauma and the child is not sexually active. Examination shows erythema of the vulva and a thin, non-blood-stained whitish discharge with no skin thickening or lesions. Which is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Non-specific prepubertal vulvovaginitis is the most common gynecological condition in prepubertal girls. The lack of estrogen makes the prepubertal vaginal epithelium thin and susceptible to irritation by poor hygiene (back-to-front wiping), soaps, and constipation. The presentation — mild discharge, pruritus, and erythema without skin lesions or specific organisms — fits non-specific vulvovaginitis. Candidiasis is uncommon in prepubertal girls without risk factors. Foreign body typically causes unilateral, foul-smelling, sometimes blood-stained discharge. Labial adhesions cause obstructed voiding, not discharge.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vulvovaginal candidiasis is uncommon before puberty in the absence of antibiotic use, diabetes, or immunosuppression; the hypoestrogenic prepubertal environment does not support Candida colonisation well.
Labial adhesions present with urinary stream abnormalities or post-void dribbling and partial fusion of labia minora on examination, not vaginal discharge.
Non-specific prepubertal vulvovaginitis is correct — poor perineal hygiene (back-to-front wiping), hypoestrogenic thin mucosa, and no specific pathogen explains this classic presentation.

Sexual abuse must always be considered when prepubertal children present with genital complaints, but should be suspected specifically when there are unexplained injuries, specific STIs, or disclosure; this presentation with a clear hygiene explanation does not mandate this diagnosis primarily.
Vaginal foreign body typically presents with persistent unilateral, purulent, foul-smelling, or blood-stained vaginal discharge; the history here does not suggest this.
clinicalPearl: Non-specific vulvovaginitis is the commonest gynaecological condition in prepubertal girls; the combination of hypoestrogenic thin mucosa and poor perineal hygiene is usually causative.
keyClue: Age 5 + back-to-front wiping habit + non-specific thin whitish discharge and vulval erythema = non-specific prepubertal vulvovaginitis.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing candidiasis — Candida is rare in prepubertal girls without predisposing factors; always consider the hypoestrogenic environment.
missedConcept: The prepubertal hypoestrogenic vaginal epithelium is uniquely susceptible to non-specific irritation, making vulvovaginitis the most common childhood gynecological problem.

sourceLecture: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation
sourceFile: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation.pdf
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman is on day 3 post-emergency caesarean section for failure to progress. She develops a temperature of 38.6°C. She reports lower abdominal pain and a foul-smelling lochia. On examination, her uterus is tender and larger than expected for day 3. Her WBC is 18,000 cells/µL. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Wound infection of the caesarean incision
explanation: Puerperal endometritis is the most common cause of post-caesarean fever appearing on days 2–5. The classic triad is fever, uterine tenderness (subinvolution), and foul-smelling lochia. Risk factors include caesarean section, prolonged labour, and internal monitoring. The tender, subinvoluted uterus and offensive lochia are the distinguishing features here. Wound infection typically presents later (days 5–7) at the incision site. UTI presents with dysuria, frequency, and costovertebral tenderness. Mastitis appears after day 10 with breast findings. PE presents with respiratory symptoms and dyspnea.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pulmonary embolism presents with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritis, tachycardia, and hypoxia — not uterine tenderness and foul lochia.
Caesarean wound infection typically manifests on days 5–7 with localised incision erythema, swelling, and wound discharge, not uterine tenderness and offensive lochia.
Puerperal endometritis is correct — fever + tender subinvoluted uterus + offensive lochia on post-caesarean day 3 is the textbook triad.
Urinary tract infection causes fever, dysuria, urinary frequency, and costovertebral angle tenderness; it does not explain uterine tenderness or lochia changes.
Mastitis occurs typically after day 10 postpartum and presents with unilateral breast engorgement, erythema, and pain in a lactating woman, not uterine findings.
clinicalPearl: Endometritis is the most common cause of post-caesarean fever (days 2–5): tender subinvoluted uterus + offensive lochia + fever = endometritis until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Post-caesarean day 3 + fever + tender subinvoluted uterus + foul-smelling lochia = puerperal endometritis.
commonExamTrap: Attributing all post-caesarean fever to wound infection, which typically presents later (days 5–7) at the skin incision site.
missedConcept: Timing and localising signs are key: endometritis (uterine tenderness, lochia) peaks earlier than wound infection (incision site, later onset).

sourceLecture: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation
sourceFile: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation.pdf
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman, 18 days after a normal vaginal delivery, presents to the emergency department with heavy vaginal bleeding that has soaked three pads in 2 hours. She also has mild lower abdominal cramps and a low-grade fever of 37.9°C. On bimanual examination, her uterus is soft and mildly enlarged. Her serum hCG is 180 IU/L. Which investigation is MOST helpful in confirming the cause of her bleeding?
options: Colour Doppler ultrasound of uterine arteries
explanation: Secondary postpartum haemorrhage occurring 24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum with a mildly elevated hCG, subinvoluted uterus, and low-grade fever is highly suggestive of retained products of conception (RPOC) with secondary endometritis. Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation and will typically demonstrate echogenic intracavitary tissue with or without vascularity on Doppler. It guides management (conservative, medical, or surgical evacuation). CT pelvis is not first-line. Endometrial biopsy would be premature. Uterine artery Doppler assesses vascularity but is not a standalone diagnostic tool for RPOC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT pelvis with contrast is not first-line for suspected RPOC; it exposes the patient to unnecessary radiation when TVS is highly informative and non-invasive.
Serum progesterone has no role in diagnosing secondary PPH or retained products.
Transvaginal ultrasound is correct — it directly visualises the uterine cavity, identifies echogenic intracavitary material consistent with RPOC, and assesses vascularity with Doppler.
Endometrial biopsy is not indicated as a first-line investigation and risks exacerbating bleeding or introducing further infection before imaging is obtained.
Colour Doppler of uterine arteries is useful for diagnosing arteriovenous malformations, not primarily for RPOC; TVS is the initial investigation of choice.
clinicalPearl: Secondary PPH (>24h to 12 weeks postpartum) with subinvolution and mildly elevated hCG = suspect retained products of conception; transvaginal ultrasound is the first investigation.
keyClue: Day 18 postpartum + heavy bleeding + subinvoluted uterus + mildly elevated hCG = retained products of conception.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT or MRI before TVS for suspected RPOC — TVS is cheaper, non-invasive, readily available, and highly informative.
missedConcept: Distinguishing secondary PPH causes: RPOC (ultrasound shows intracavitary echogenic material) vs. endometritis alone (empty cavity, fever predominates).

sourceLecture: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation
sourceFile: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation.pdf
clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman delivered her third child 6 weeks ago following a complicated delivery with an estimated blood loss of 2.5 litres and a period of hypotension requiring IV fluid resuscitation. She was unable to breastfeed because she never produced any milk. She now presents with fatigue, cold intolerance, loss of pubic and axillary hair, and amenorrhea. She has no headache or visual field changes. On examination, she appears pale and lethargic. Which is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Sheehan syndrome is pituitary infarction or necrosis following severe obstetric haemorrhage and hypotension. The pituitary gland enlarges during pregnancy and is susceptible to ischaemia when blood flow is compromised. Failure of lactation (prolactin deficiency) immediately postpartum is the earliest and most specific sign. Secondary hypothyroidism (TSH deficiency), secondary hypoadrenalism (ACTH deficiency), and secondary hypogonadism (FSH/LH deficiency) produce the constellation of fatigue, cold intolerance, and amenorrhea. Prolactinoma causes excess prolactin, not failure of lactation. Primary hypothyroidism would not explain failure of lactation or hair loss pattern.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Postpartum depression is a psychiatric diagnosis; it does not cause failure of lactation, hair loss, or hormonal deficiency.
Prolactinoma causes excess prolactin with galactorrhea and amenorrhea from hyperprolactinaemia; it would not cause lactation failure — the opposite would be true.

Primary hypothyroidism causes elevated TSH; it does not explain failure of lactation, loss of pubic hair, or the history of massive obstetric haemorrhage — secondary hypothyroidism from pituitary failure is the mechanism here.
Sheehan syndrome is correct — massive postpartum haemorrhage with hypotension → pituitary ischaemia → pan-hypopituitarism; failure to lactate is the hallmark early sign.

Lymphocytic hypophysitis is an autoimmune pituitary inflammation seen mostly in late pregnancy or early puerperium without the dramatic haemorrhagic precipitant; it is far less likely than Sheehan syndrome given this history.
clinicalPearl: Failure to lactate after massive obstetric haemorrhage is the earliest and most specific clue to Sheehan syndrome (pituitary necrosis); subsequent pan-hypopituitarism develops over weeks to months.
keyClue: Massive PPH + hypotension → failure of lactation + fatigue + amenorrhea + cold intolerance + hair loss = Sheehan syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing primary hypothyroidism — the multi-system deficiency (prolactin, TSH, gonadotropins) points to a central (pituitary) lesion, not a primary thyroid problem.
missedConcept: Failure to lactate postpartum is not simply 'low milk supply' — in the context of obstetric haemorrhage it is a red flag for Sheehan syndrome and pan-hypopituitarism.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old primigravida at 39+2 weeks presents with painful uterine contractions for the past 14 hours. On admission, her cervix was 2 cm dilated. On repeat examination 10 hours later, the cervix is 3 cm dilated with mild effacement. Contractions occur every 8–10 minutes and last 25–30 seconds. The fetal CTG is reassuring and the head is engaged at -1 station. What is the MOST ACCURATE description of the current labour status?
explanation: The latent phase of labour extends from the onset of regular contractions to active labour (typically 6 cm in current guidelines, though some definitions use 4 cm). A prolonged latent phase in a primigravida is defined as lasting more than 20 hours. This patient has been in labour for 24 hours without reaching active labour (cervix is only 3 cm despite 24 hours of contractions), confirming prolonged latent phase. Active phase arrest implies failure to progress after reaching active labour (≥6 cm). Secondary arrest of descent applies to the second stage. The contractions are also infrequent and mild.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Normal active phase requires regular contractions and cervical dilation of at least 6 cm progressing at ≥0.5–1 cm/hour; at 3 cm after 24 hours this is not active phase.
Active phase arrest is failure to progress after entering active labour (≥6 cm); this patient has not yet reached active labour.
Prolonged latent phase is correct — >20 hours in a primigravida without reaching active labour, with infrequent and mild contractions and minimal cervical change over 10 hours.
Secondary arrest of descent refers to arrest of fetal head descent in the second stage; this patient is still in the first stage.
Precipitate labour is an abnormally rapid labour (<3 hours from onset to delivery); the opposite is true here.
clinicalPearl: Prolonged latent phase in a primigravida = >20 hours without reaching active labour; infrequent, irregular, and weak contractions are characteristic.
keyClue: 24 hours of contractions, cervix only 3 cm, infrequent mild contractions, primigravida = prolonged latent phase.
commonExamTrap: Labelling this 'active phase arrest' — arrest implies failure of progress WITHIN active labour (≥6 cm); this patient has not entered active labour.
missedConcept: Distinguishing latent phase from active phase: active labour begins at ≥6 cm; problems before that point are latent phase disorders.

clinicalStem: A 30-year-old multigravida at 40+1 weeks presents with regular painful contractions every 3–4 minutes. On vaginal examination, the cervix is fully dilated (10 cm), fully effaced, and the fetal head is at +1 station with an anterior fontanelle felt to the left. She is pushing with contractions but after 45 minutes of active pushing the head has not advanced and remains at +1 station. Which is the MOST ACCURATE description of the current obstetric problem?
options: Second stage of labour — normal progress
Arrest of descent in the second stage
Failure to progress in the first stage
Obstructed labour due to absolute cephalopelvic disproportion
explanation: Full cervical dilation defines entry into the second stage of labour. Active pushing has occurred for 45 minutes without descent of the presenting part (head remains at +1 station). In a multigravida, the second stage is expected to progress with descent; lack of descent after 30–60 minutes of active pushing raises concern for arrest of descent, which warrants assessment for malposition, malpresentation, or cephalopelvic disproportion. The diagnosis is not yet obstructed labour (which requires clinical signs of obstructed labour over a longer time course). Prolonged latent phase and first stage problems do not apply at full dilation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Normal progress in the second stage would show consistent fetal descent with each contraction; 45 minutes of pushing without advancement is not normal progress in a multigravida.
Prolonged latent phase is a first-stage disorder defined by delayed entry into active labour; it is irrelevant once the cervix is fully dilated.
Arrest of descent in the second stage is correct — full dilation + active pushing for 45 minutes + no descent = arrest of descent requiring assessment for cause.
Failure to progress in the first stage refers to inadequate cervical dilation during active labour; once the cervix is fully dilated, this terminology no longer applies.
Obstructed labour is a clinical diagnosis requiring signs such as a bandl ring, caput succedaneum, and moulding over a prolonged time course; 45 minutes of arrest alone does not confirm absolute CPD.
clinicalPearl: Arrest of descent = failure of the presenting part to advance despite adequate contractions and active pushing after full cervical dilation; always assess for malposition, malrotation, or CPD.
keyClue: Full cervical dilation + active pushing 45 minutes + no change in station = arrest of descent in the second stage.
commonExamTrap: Immediately diagnosing 'obstructed labour' — this requires additional signs (bandl ring, severe moulding, caput) over a longer duration and is not based on 45 minutes of arrested descent alone.
missedConcept: The second stage has its own distinct labour abnormalities (arrest of descent, prolonged second stage); these must be distinguished from first-stage disorders.

clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl is referred to the clinic with primary amenorrhea. She reports cyclical lower abdominal pain every month for the past 6 months that lasts 2–3 days. On examination, she has Tanner stage 4 breast development and pubic hair. There is a bulging, bluish-tinged, translucent membrane visible at the vaginal introitus. Her mother reports that the girl has been missing school each month due to the pain. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: This is the classic presentation of imperforate hymen: a young girl with normal pubertal development (Tanner stage 4) experiencing cyclical pelvic pain (cryptomenorrhea) and primary amenorrhea. The pathognomonic finding is the bluish, bulging, translucent hymenal membrane caused by haematocolpos — blood accumulating behind the obstruction. MRKH syndrome has vaginal agenesis (no visible membrane), no cyclical pain, and no outflow obstruction. Transverse vaginal septum is higher in the vaginal canal and may not present with the classic visible bulge at the introitus. Androgen insensitivity presents with sparse pubic hair and 46,XY karyotype.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRKH syndrome (vaginal agenesis) presents with primary amenorrhea and absence of the vaginal canal; there is no bulging membrane at the introitus, and cyclical pain is absent because there is no functional endometrium.
Imperforate hymen is correct — the bluish bulging translucent membrane at the introitus with cyclical pain and primary amenorrhea = haematocolpos from imperforate hymen; this is pathognomonic.
Transverse vaginal septum lies higher in the vaginal canal and does not produce a visible bulge at the introitus; it also causes cyclical pain and haematocolpos but the exam finding is distinct.
Labial adhesions can partially obscure the vaginal opening but do not produce a bluish bulging translucent hymenal membrane; they are also a childhood problem that typically resolves with puberty.
Androgen insensitivity syndrome presents with a 46,XY female phenotype, absent or sparse pubic and axillary hair, and a blind-ending vaginal pouch with no uterus — not a bulging hymenal membrane.
clinicalPearl: Bluish bulging translucent membrane at the introitus + cyclical pelvic pain + primary amenorrhea = imperforate hymen with haematocolpos — pathognomonic; treat with hymenotomy.
keyClue: Bluish bulging translucent membrane at the introitus + cyclical pain + normal pubertal development = haematocolpos from imperforate hymen.
commonExamTrap: Confusing with MRKH syndrome — MRKH has no visible hymenal bulge, no cyclical pain, and no outflow tract obstruction.
missedConcept: Primary amenorrhea with normal puberty and cyclical pain always suggests an outflow obstruction — examine the introitus carefully for a bulging hymen.
